headline_of_the_day: Hacker wipes Romania's entire land registry database in active cyberattack

key_points.text: A hacker wiped Romania's land registry database, disrupting property records for an entire country with immediate consequences for real estate transactions and ownership verification.
Russian intelligence hijacked security cameras across Europe and Ukraine to spy on military logistics, NATO operations, and weapons shipments bound for Kyiv.
Hackers stole significant data from Craneware, a tech firm used by thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies for billing, potentially exposing health information.
US gas prices climbed back to $4 a gallon as the US and Iran launched attacks, pushing oil prices over 15 percent higher in one week.
The EU fined AliExpress $629 million for failing to remove illegal and counterfeit products, the largest penalty under the new Digital Services Act.

stories.title: Amazon breaks Apple Wallet feature used by millions
stories.summary: A change on Amazon's side disabled a widely used Apple Wallet capability, affecting how customers access digital tickets and passes.

stories.title: AMD launches Helios rack system to compete with Nvidia, Microsoft buys in
stories.summary: The chip maker shipped its first complete artificial intelligence rack system with Microsoft as the first major customer, marking a concrete product in the datacenter AI hardware race.

article.background: AMD makes computer chips that power data centers and artificial intelligence systems. Nvidia has dominated this market for years. AMD has been developing its own AI chip systems to compete. Now AMD has announced Helios, its first complete AI rack system, which is a unit of hardware designed to work together in large computing centers.
article.what_happened: AMD launched Helios, a rack system for AI work, with Microsoft as its first major customer. Microsoft said it will deploy AMD's next-generation Instinct chips (for AI) and EPYC chips (for general computing) in its Azure cloud service. This expands the existing partnership between the two companies.
article.why_it_matters: More competition in AI chips can push companies to innovate faster and keep prices down. Microsoft's choice to buy from AMD shows that Nvidia faces real alternatives in the market. This matters to anyone using cloud AI services, since more choices among chip makers help control what those services cost.

stories.title: Archer and Anduril unveil military electric air taxi
stories.summary: The two companies jointly developed an autonomous vertical takeoff aircraft for defense applications, moving electric air taxis from concept to military deployment.

article.background: Archer Aviation builds electric aircraft that take off and land vertically, like helicopters. Anduril Industries is a defense contractor. The two companies have partnered to adapt Archer's technology for military use.
article.what_happened: Anduril and Archer jointly unveiled Thunder, an autonomous military aircraft built on their shared platform. The aircraft is designed to operate alongside helicopters in military operations, supporting them in the field. Archer's stock rose 18% after the announcement.
article.why_it_matters: The companies are using electric aircraft technology for military purposes, not just commercial passenger transport. This means air taxi companies may earn stable funding from defense contracts alongside their civilian businesses.

stories.title: Trump administration moves to restrict access to frontier AI models
stories.summary: The government is working to control who can access the most advanced artificial intelligence systems domestically, a shift with direct implications for researchers and companies.

stories.title: AI system disproves 87-year-old math conjecture in hours
stories.summary: A mathematician used Claude to resolve the Jacobian Conjecture, a theoretical problem that had stumped researchers for nearly nine decades, demonstrating genuine capability in mathematical reasoning.

stories.title: United employee threatens to call immigration enforcement on passenger at San Francisco airport
stories.summary: A United Airlines agent at SFO threatened to contact ICE during a ticketing dispute with a Mexican American passenger, raising questions about company training and treatment of customers.

article.background: United Airlines operates flights from San Francisco International Airport, one of the Bay Area's major hubs. A ticketing dispute between a passenger and a United employee can escalate quickly when tensions rise during the check-in process.
article.what_happened: A United Airlines agent at SFO threatened to call ICE, the federal immigration enforcement agency, during a disagreement with a San Ramon passenger over a ticket issue. The agent made remarks suggesting the passenger did not act like a citizen. United launched an investigation into the incident after it was reported by multiple news outlets.
article.why_it_matters: When airline staff invoke immigration enforcement during routine customer disputes, it affects how safe passengers feel traveling. This incident raises questions about what conduct airlines will tolerate from their employees and what standards govern their interactions with customers.

stories.title: FDA retracts test result but still links Taylor Farms lettuce to parasite outbreak
stories.summary: The agency walked back a positive finding for Cyclospora in lettuce but maintains the Taylor Farms product is connected to an outbreak affecting multiple states and restaurant chains.

article.background: Cyclosporiasis is a foodborne illness caused by a parasite found in contaminated produce. Taylor Farms is a major lettuce supplier whose products are used by restaurants across the country. The company became the focus of an investigation after several cases of the illness were linked to its lettuce.
article.what_happened: The FDA retracted one test result that initially showed the cyclospora parasite in Taylor Farms lettuce, determining it was a false positive. However, the agency still links Taylor Farms lettuce to the outbreak based on other evidence. Iceberg lettuce from the company has been recalled across 27 states, affecting major restaurant chains that use lettuce in salads.
article.why_it_matters: The outbreak has caused some people to avoid salads at popular chains. The FDA's retraction shows the agency is being careful about its findings, but the investigation continues, so eating out at affected restaurants carries a small risk during the outbreak.

stories.title: US gas prices return to $4 a gallon as US and Iran launch attacks
stories.summary: Pump prices climbed back above four dollars per gallon nationwide after oil surged more than 15 percent in a week amid escalating Middle East fighting.

stories.title: Oil prices touch $90 despite five months of US-Iran war
stories.summary: Brent crude topped 90 dollars a barrel as Houthi rebels threaten shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, though prices remain lower than many analysts expected given the conflict's duration.

stories.title: EU fines AliExpress $629 million for illegal product sales
stories.summary: The European Commission imposed a record penalty after the platform failed to comply with previous orders to stop selling counterfeit and unsafe goods, the largest fine under the Digital Services Act.

article.background: AliExpress is an online marketplace where sellers from around the world list products for sale. It is owned by Alibaba Group, a Chinese company. The European Union passed a law in 2022 called the Digital Services Act, which requires online marketplaces to stop illegal sales on their platforms.
article.what_happened: The European Commission, the EU's executive branch, fined AliExpress €550 million, or about $629 million. This is the largest penalty ever imposed under the Digital Services Act. The fine came after AliExpress failed to follow earlier orders to remove counterfeit and unsafe products from its site.
article.why_it_matters: This fine signals that the EU will enforce its product safety rules with major penalties. For shoppers, it means platforms will likely invest more in checking what sellers offer, which may affect prices and selection. It also shows that even large global companies must comply with EU safety standards.

stories.title: Czechia to ban mobile phones in schools starting September 2027
stories.summary: The government set a firm implementation date for a nationwide school phone ban affecting millions of students.

stories.title: Five cups of coffee per day safe for most adults, study confirms
stories.summary: A large study found up to 400 milligrams of caffeine daily does not pose health risks for the majority of people, confirming widely held assumptions about coffee consumption.

stories.title: LED lighting squanders potential to reduce light pollution
stories.summary: Despite energy efficiency gains, widespread LED adoption has not reduced nighttime sky brightness due to poor implementation choices affecting wildlife and astronomy.

stories.title: Hacker wipes Romania's land registry database
stories.summary: An active cyberattack destroyed the national property records system, halting real estate transactions across the country.

stories.title: Russian intelligence hacks European security cameras to spy on NATO military logistics
stories.summary: Dutch intelligence confirmed that Russian services are systematically hijacking internet-connected cameras to watch weapons shipments and troop movements in Ukraine and NATO states.

stories.title: Healthcare tech firm breach exposes data from thousands of US hospitals
stories.summary: Edinburgh-based Craneware, which makes billing software for hospitals and pharmacies, confirmed hackers stole a significant amount of customer data including potential health records.

stories.title: Hackers exploit recently patched WordPress bugs, millions of sites at risk
stories.summary: Two critical flaws gave hackers remote takeover capability on tens of millions of WordPress websites whose owners have not yet updated.
